prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 29-year-old Black woman.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had routine bloodwork and feel fine. I had a bad cold about 2 weeks ago that has resolved. I don't have nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in my urine/stool, or unusual bruising.
 - History: No blood thinners or new medications.
 - Vitals: BP 112/70, HR 74
 - Exam: No abnormal exam findings reported.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Platelets: 128 x10^3/uL (150-400) LOW
 - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - WBC: 6.8 x10^3/uL (4.5-11.0) normal
 - INR: 1.0 (0.9-1.1) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your platelet count is mildly low at 128, just below the normal range of 150-400. Everything else — hemoglobin, white blood cells, and clotting (INR) — looks perfectly normal. Importantly, you have no symptoms of bleeding or bruising, your vitals are healthy, and you feel fine.

A very common cause of a mildly low platelet count is a recent viral infection, like the bad cold you had two weeks ago. Viruses can temporarily suppress platelet production or increase their turnover. In many cases, the count bounces back to normal on its own within a few weeks.

This level (128) is not low enough to cause spontaneous bleeding — that typically doesn't happen until platelets drop well below 50. However, it's worth having your doctor review this and likely recheck your platelets in a few weeks to confirm they're trending back to normal.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 90%

A very common and well-recognized cause of a mildly low platelet count is a recent viral infection, like the bad cold you had about 2 weeks ago. Viruses can temporarily suppress platelet production or cause mild immune-mediated platelet destruction. In most cases like this, the count recovers on its own within a few weeks. This level (128) is not low enough to cause spontaneous bleeding (that risk typically arises below 50 or so). Your doctor will likely want to recheck your platelets in a few weeks to confirm they're trending back to normal.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 90%

Importantly, all your other blood counts (hemoglobin, white blood cells) and your clotting test (INR) are completely normal, your vitals are healthy, and you have no symptoms of abnormal bleeding or bruising. A very common and benign cause of a mildly low platelet count is a recent viral illness — your bad cold two weeks ago could easily explain this temporary dip. There is nothing here suggesting an emergency.
